rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is an acute flare of a KNOWN chronic autoimmune condition (SLE) with a NEGATIVE acute-red-flag screen: symmetrical non-hot polyarthritis (no septic joint), a symmetrical photosensitive malar rash (not cellulitis), only a low-grade fever, and no pleuritic chest pain or breathlessness (no serositis). The source clinician's own disposition was routine outpatient (GP + rheumatology review in 5 days). The correct telehealth answer is prompt GP review (24-72 h) for a corticosteroid course and bloods/urinalysis plus rheumatology follow-up — T2 (prompt GP) — nudged from a purely routine T1 by the severity of the flare, the need for a prescription steroid, and the social vulnerability of a patient living alone who cannot currently prepare food. It is NOT an emergency: over-triage to ED/000 for the flare itself is the primary scored defect. Escalation to T4/T5 applies only if a red-flag complication (septic joint, serositis, nephritis, CNS involvement) develops.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A single joint becomes disproportionately swollen, hot and red, much worse than the others
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — attend the emergency department for urgent joint assessment and possible aspiration
escalation_edges.rationale: A dominant hot monoarticular joint in an immunosuppressed patient may be septic arthritis, which requires urgent aspiration and antibiotics to prevent joint destruction and sepsis.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If any one joint suddenly becomes much more swollen, hot and red than the rest, go to the emergency department the same day — this needs an urgent check for joint infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Sharp, stabbing chest pain when breathing in, or sudden shortness of breath
escalation_edges.rationale: Pleuritic chest pain or breathlessness may signal lupus serositis (pleuritis/pericarditis) or a pulmonary embolism (SLE carries a prothrombotic risk) — both life-threatening and needing emergency assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get sharp chest pain when you breathe in, or you suddenly can't catch your breath, call 000 straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Reduced urine output, frothy urine, or new swelling of the ankles or face
escalation_edges.rationale: These suggest lupus nephritis / renal flare, which needs urgent urinalysis, bloods and specialist review to protect kidney function.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you notice you're passing much less urine, your urine looks frothy, or your ankles or face swell up, get an urgent same-day medical review.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Severe unremitting headache, confusion, drowsiness, seizure or new weakness/numbness
escalation_edges.rationale: Neuropsychiatric lupus (CNS involvement) is a medical emergency requiring immediate hospital assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a severe headache that won't settle, become confused or very drowsy, have a seizure, or notice new weakness or numbness, call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Unable to keep fluids down, unable to care for self at home, or the flare worsens despite starting treatment
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same-day contact with GP or after-hours service
escalation_edges.rationale: A patient living alone who cannot maintain hydration or basic self-care during a severe flare needs an earlier, escalated review and possible supported care, even without a specific red flag.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't keep fluids down, can't look after yourself at home, or things get worse despite treatment, contact your GP or an after-hours doctor the same day.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives alone and is currently unable to reach the kitchen or prepare food because of hand and joint pain
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold for an earlier, in-person review and arrange practical support (someone to check in, help with food/hydration). The isolation is part of why baseline sits at T2 rather than a purely routine T1, and it strengthens ESC-005.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Immunosuppressed (hydroxychloroquine, and about to start corticosteroids) with a low-grade fever
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Keep a lower threshold for infection: an intercurrent infection or a septic joint must be excluded before/alongside escalating immunosuppression, and any high-spiking fever or dominant hot joint should escalate promptly.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like a flare of your known lupus set off by the sun, not an emergency. Arrange a GP review in the next day or two to start a short steroid course and check your bloods and urine, and ask for a rheumatology review within about a week. Keep taking your hydroxychloroquine, use high-SPF sunscreen and stay out of the sun, and use paracetamol for pain. Watch for the warning signs below and escalate if any appear.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Book a prompt GP appointment (within 24-72 hours); use an after-hours GP service if you deteriorate outside clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If a warning sign appears after hours, call your after-hours GP or healthdirect (1800 022 222); for chest pain on breathing, sudden breathlessness, a severe headache/confusion or a single hot swollen joint, do not wait — call 000 or go to the emergency department.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth-assessed acute SLE flare (mucocutaneous/articular): symmetrical polyarthritis, photosensitive malar rash, fatigue, low-grade fever after sun exposure; acute-red-flag screen negative (no dominant hot joint, no pleuritic pain/breathlessness). Requests: corticosteroid course, FBC/ESR/CRP/anti-dsDNA/complement/creatinine, urinalysis for nephritis, continue hydroxychloroquine, rheumatology review.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the flare as pure self-care with no prescriber review and no safety-netting, missing the need for a steroid course and the screen for serositis/nephritis/septic joint.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An untreated flare progresses, or an evolving septic joint or lupus nephritis is missed, causing joint destruction or irreversible renal damage.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Treating it as a routine 1-2 week GP appointment without safety-netting, given the severity, the immunosuppression/fever and the social isolation — an unsupported patient living alone could deteriorate before review.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Sending an uncomplicated lupus flare with a negative acute-red-flag screen to urgent care, ED or 000 (the primary scored defect this over-triage-resistance case tests) — unnecessary emergency attendance, infection exposure for an immunosuppressed patient, and eroded trust when prompt outpatient management was correct.
